decisions.description: REVISED per FOUND-02: Vercel cron scanner as scheduling layer — no dedicated scheduler service. Cron hits /api/cron/scan every 1 min, scans agent_triggers + autopilot_config for due items, fires /api/trigger/run. The LLM tool loop IS the workflow engine — no Temporal, no Inngest, no DAGs.
decisions.decision: Vercel cron scanner replaces Trigger.dev. LLM tool loop as workflow engine confirmed.
decisions.reasoning: Per FOUND-02 revision: Trigger.dev dropped from v1. Scheduling layer is now Vercel cron (1-min interval) → /api/cron/scan route → queries agent_triggers WHERE next_fire_at <= NOW() AND enabled = true + autopilot_config WHERE next_pulse_at <= NOW() AND autopilot_enabled = true → fires /api/trigger/run with payload. Same pattern as Fintool (CloudWatch every 10 min → Lambda → query next_run_at) and Tasklet (platform-managed trigger system). The LLM tool loop as workflow engine is the real architecture decision: agent reads .md instruction file (RUNNER-07), executes via generateText with tools, no state machines or DAG definitions needed.

decisions.description: REVISED (v2) per FOUND-02 + idempotency review: Vercel cron scanner at /api/cron/scan, every 1 min. Scanner uses atomic UPDATE...RETURNING with claim token to prevent duplicate dispatch. Query: UPDATE agent_triggers SET current_run_id = gen_random_uuid(), last_fired_at = NOW(), next_fire_at = compute_next(cron_expression) WHERE next_fire_at <= NOW() AND enabled = true AND current_run_id IS NULL RETURNING *. Second scanner tick sees current_run_id IS NOT NULL, skips. Same atomic pattern for autopilot_config. Fires /api/trigger/run per claimed item with { clientId, triggerId, triggerType, runId: current_run_id }. Execution route validates claim: SELECT WHERE id = triggerId AND current_run_id = runId — abort if no match (stale dispatch). On completion: SET current_run_id = NULL (releases claim). Stale claim reaper runs in same scanner: UPDATE agent_triggers SET current_run_id = NULL WHERE current_run_id IS NOT NULL AND last_fired_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '15 minutes'. Schema addition: current_run_id UUID DEFAULT NULL on agent_triggers. Lightweight — no LLM calls in scanner, just DB queries + HTTP dispatch.
decisions.decision: REVISED: Atomic claim via UPDATE...RETURNING + current_run_id column. Prevents duplicate dispatch at DB level. Stale claims reaped after 15 min. Vercel cron every 1 min.
decisions.reasoning: REVISED after idempotency review. Original said 'scanner must be idempotent' but had no mechanism — just a comment about last_scan_at. Three failure modes identified: (1) Scanner overlap — scan at :00 dispatches trigger, run takes 90s, scan at :01 dispatches same trigger again. (2) Retry collision — scanner re-picks a 'failed' trigger while original is still running. (3) Vercel function replay — replayed /api/trigger/run executes trigger twice. Atomic UPDATE...RETURNING eliminates all three: only one scanner wins the UPDATE, second gets zero rows. current_run_id passed to execution route as idempotency key — runs table has unique constraint on run_id, so replays fail on INSERT. Stale claim reaper (15-min threshold matching Vercel Fluid Compute ceiling per GAP-17) handles crashed functions that never released their claim. Confirmed with Tasklet dev: Tasklet's mechanism is opaque but zero duplicates observed in production. Tasklet dev recommended A+B (atomic claim + idempotent tool calls) for systems where duplicates have real consequences (emails, CRM writes). Fintool uses triple-layer defense (SQS dedup + Temporal exactly-once + processed-pair tracking) — overkill for our scale. Cost: one column, one WHERE clause change in scanner, ~30 lines of code.

decisions.description: Agent-created triggers as first-class feature — agent calls create_trigger tool → INSERT into agent_triggers table → scanner picks up on next tick → fires /api/trigger/run. No deploy needed for new automations.
decisions.decision: Confirmed. Runtime trigger creation via DB writes, no deploy needed.
decisions.reasoning: Core Tasklet feature (setup_trigger tool). Agent creates automations by writing to agent_triggers table at runtime. Scanner picks up any new trigger on next 1-min tick. User says 'remind me every Monday to check listings' → agent creates trigger → it just works. No code changes, no redeployment. This is what makes the agent feel autonomous — it sets up its own workflows. The trigger row points to a workflow .md file (RUNNER-07) that a fresh LLM interprets at fire time. Revised from 'fires agent-trigger-run task' to 'fires /api/trigger/run' per FOUND-02 (no Trigger.dev).

decisions.description: REVISED: agent_triggers table schema — id (uuid PK), client_id (FK, RLS), thread_id (uuid FK, NOT NULL — trigger fires INTO this thread), trigger_type (schedule/event), name (text), cron_expression (nullable, schedule only), event_source (nullable, event only), instruction_path (text, NOT NULL — reference doc for complex workflows, insurance against compaction), payload (jsonb, nullable), enabled (boolean), next_fire_at (timestamptz), last_fired_at (nullable), last_status (nullable), created_at, updated_at. Triggers fire into the thread where they were created (Tasklet-confirmed behavior). Trigger event is inserted as a thread_message (role: 'system') using Tasklet structured XML format: <system-message><trigger-event> with trigger-name, trigger-title, event-title, trigger-instance-id, integration-id, payload. Cron expression is NOT included in event payload (LLM only sees it via manage_active_triggers tool). The LLM receives full thread history including prior trigger events and user replies. instruction_path .md file serves as detailed reference doc the agent CAN read, not sole context.
decisions.decision: Triggers fire into their creation thread. thread_id NOT NULL on agent_triggers. Trigger events as structured XML thread_messages. instruction_path as reference doc. One execution path for chat and triggers.
decisions.reasoning: REVISED based on Tasklet live confirmation: triggers fire as system messages into the conversation where they were created, not as isolated subagent runs. Confirmed by creating 3 triggers in one Tasklet conversation and observing all 3 fire back into that same conversation as <system-message><trigger-event> XML. The LLM receives full thread history (including prior trigger events and user replies) — no isolated sessions, no marked runs, no reply routing. User can reply naturally to trigger results because it is the same conversation. instruction_path remains NOT NULL as insurance: when conversation compaction loses setup details, the .md file preserves detailed workflow instructions. Trigger event format follows Tasklet verbatim: structured XML with trigger-name (type), trigger-title (human label), event-title (event description), trigger-instance-id (for management), integration-id (system vs connection), and payload. Cron expression deliberately excluded from event payload (Tasklet pattern). Key drift from prior design: thread_id added, execution model changed from isolated subagent to in-thread conversation turn.

decisions.description: REVISED per FOUND-02: No separate task types. Two API routes handle all execution: /api/cron/scan (scanner, lightweight DB queries + HTTP dispatch, runs every 1 min) and /api/trigger/run (execution, reads instruction_path, runs subagent, up to 15-min Vercel Fluid Compute cap). Timeout is uniform — everything runs within 15 min. Original Trigger.dev task types (pulse-checker, autopilot-pulse, agent-trigger-run, deep-research, document-processing, enrichment-batch) collapsed into one execution route with different payloads.
decisions.decision: Two API routes replace six Trigger.dev task types. Uniform 15-min timeout.
decisions.reasoning: Per FOUND-02: Trigger.dev dropped. The six named task types were Trigger.dev constructs with per-type timeouts and concurrency settings. Without Trigger.dev, all execution is Vercel serverless functions with a uniform 15-min cap. The scanner route is lightweight (DB queries only). The execution route handles everything — autopilot pulses, agent triggers, background tasks — differentiated by the trigger row's instruction_path and payload, not by separate task type definitions. If anything genuinely needs >15 min, it's a candidate for v3 Trigger.dev reintroduction per FOUND-02.

decisions.description: REVISED: Concurrency model — per-THREAD serialization (one active run at a time per thread_id). Trigger events and user messages share the same per-thread queue. Different threads may run concurrently with NO cross-thread cap in v1. Cross-client unlimited (Vercel scales horizontally). If trigger fires while thread is busy → trigger event queues behind current run (same pending_messages mechanism as chat). If user types while trigger is running in same thread → user message queues. Per-thread lock releases when run reaches any terminal status (completed/partial/failed/cancelled) per RUNNER-08. Stale run cleanup: runs older than 15 min still 'running' → mark 'failed'. DB-enforced via runs table (serverless-compatible). Add configurable per-client cap in v2 only if cost data warrants it.
decisions.decision: Per-thread serialization. Cross-thread uncapped in v1. DB-enforced. Add cap later if needed.
decisions.reasoning: REVISED: Dropped the hard cap of 2 concurrent trigger runs across threads. A real estate agent will have 3-5 triggers total — the chance of all firing in the same minute is low. Per-thread serialization is the hard rule (prevents conflicts within a conversation). Cross-thread parallelism is safe because each thread is an independent conversation with its own context. YAGNI — add a configurable per-client cap in v2 only if cost data shows runaway execution. Researched Dorabot (no global cap), OpenClaw (global cap of 4), Tasklet (not documented). Dorabot's no-cap approach is appropriate for v1 given our low trigger volume per client.

decisions.description: Platform notification email — agent has send_message tool (Tasklet parity). When agent calls send_message(to: 'owner', subject, body), platform sends HTML email from notifications@sunder.ai via Resend. Email includes: agent/thread name as header link, formatted body (markdown → HTML), link back to the thread in web app. Not every trigger sends an email — the agent decides based on whether results are noteworthy. This is platform transactional email (not user email integration, which remains Phase 3). Tasklet sends from notifications@agents.tasklet.ai with the same pattern.
decisions.decision: send_message tool → platform email via Resend from notifications@sunder.ai. Agent decides when to email. Links back to thread.
decisions.reasoning: In v1 without Telegram/WhatsApp, the user has no way to know the agent did something unless they open the app. Email bridges this gap. Tasklet screenshot confirms: agent calls send_message, platform sends formatted HTML email from notifications@agents.tasklet.ai with thread link. Sunder uses Resend (already in tech stack, deferred to Phase 3 for user email workflows, but brought forward for this lightweight platform notification use case). This is transactional email only — one sender address, no user email integration, no reply handling. The agent decides when to email (listings check finds 3 properties → email; autopilot pulse finds nothing → no email). Cost: Resend free tier covers 3,000 emails/month, more than enough for v1.

decisions.description: REVISED: Autopilot pulse config — pre-installed 'Sunder Autopilot' thread created on user signup (pinned, cannot be deleted). Autopilot trigger row auto-created pointing to this thread. System-provided autopilot instruction .md file. Config: autopilot_enabled, pulse_interval (1h/2h/6h/12h, default 6h), quiet_hours_start/end, last/next_pulse_at. All autopilot activity (trigger events, agent responses) visible in this thread. User can reply naturally to autopilot results in the same thread. Default 6h = ~4 pulses/day, consistent with TRIG-09 no-suppression-infrastructure decision.
decisions.decision: Pre-installed Autopilot thread on signup. Autopilot trigger fires into this thread. User can interact with autopilot results.
decisions.reasoning: Inspired by Dorabot's autonomy pulse (auto-created calendar item on startup) adapted to Sunder's thread model. The user should not have to set up the autopilot — it should exist from day one as a named thread. This gives the autopilot a HOME: the user knows exactly where to look for background activity. The thread accumulates autopilot history (what it checked, what it found, what it updated) so the user can scroll back and review. Replying to autopilot results ('yes, send that follow-up' or 'skip that one') works naturally because it is the same conversation. Provisioning on signup creates: (1) the thread, (2) the trigger row with thread_id, (3) the system autopilot .md file in Supabase Storage.

decisions.description: Autopilot pulse priority order and bootstrap. BOOTSTRAP: Even though autopilot fires into a thread with full history, the agent MUST call tools to get live state before acting (other threads may have changed things between pulses): tasks_view, goals_view, CRM follow-up query. Thread history = what autopilot did previously. Tool calls = what's actually true now. Both needed. PRIORITY (strict order, prompt-driven via autopilot instruction file, not hard-coded system logic): 1) Advance in_progress tasks — execute next concrete step, keep task status current. 2) Check overdue CRM follow-ups — surface what's late, draft follow-up messages for user approval. 3) Act on monitored things — check deal status changes, listing updates, anything user asked to watch. Live checks, not assumptions. 4) Follow up with the user — if they answered a previous question, incorporate it. If they haven't and it's been a while, nudge via email. 5) Handle blockers — if a task is blocked on user input, ask again. If still no answer, continue with best assumptions and log them. 6) Start next approved task — pick up next task with status planned:ready. 7) Research or prepare — if a task needs info, go get it. Check existing research first. 8) Get to know the user — if user profile is sparse, ask one concise question per pulse. 9) Engage the user — nudge about goals/tasks, remind about pending approvals/blockers/next steps. Always include a concrete next step or question. 10) Propose new goals/tasks — notice something worth doing? Create it. 11) Create momentum — break large tasks into smaller follow-up tasks and queue them. HARD RULES: Do at least one meaningful action every pulse. Do not end without a concrete next action. Before declaring 'nothing to act on', verify: goals checked, tasks checked, monitoring checked, follow-ups checked, new tasks considered. Log why none were actionable. 'Nothing to act on' should be rare. Adapted from Dorabot's 9-level autonomy pulse (verbatim philosophy), extended with CRM-specific priorities (#2, #3). Growth Plan nudging deferred to v2.
decisions.decision: Prompt-driven 11-level priority list with mandatory live-state bootstrap. Dorabot philosophy: always do something. Personal assistant slant after CRM duties cleared.
decisions.reasoning: Dorabot's autonomy pulse is production-proven: strict priority order in a prompt (not hard-coded), 'always do something' philosophy, 'nothing to act on should be rare'. Adapted for Sunder: CRM follow-ups and deal monitoring added as priorities #2-3 (Sunder-specific). Dropped Dorabot's media/meme generation (not RE CRM relevant). Key addition vs Dorabot: mandatory tool-call bootstrap even though thread history exists, because other threads can change task/CRM state between pulses. Thread history shows what autopilot did; tool calls show what's true now. Priority list lives in the autopilot instruction .md file (updatable without code changes). Fintool's 'stay silent if nothing' rejected in favor of Dorabot's 'always do something' — user wants active engagement. Tasklet has no autopilot concept (individual triggers only). Growth Plan features deferred to v2.

decisions.description: REVISED: No platform-level no-op suppression. No AUTOPILOT_OK signal. No duplicate message filtering infrastructure. Solved by frequency: autopilot default is ~4x/day (every 6 hours), not 48x/day. At this frequency each pulse has enough elapsed time for state to genuinely change, making no-op and duplicate problems rare. Handled via prompt instructions: 'keep updates concise', 'don't repeat the same reminder unless something changed.' Thread compaction (SESSION contracts) handles long-term context growth. User can adjust frequency via autopilot config (TRIG-07).
decisions.decision: No suppression infrastructure. Solve with lower pulse frequency (~4x/day) and prompt instructions.
decisions.reasoning: Original proposal had AUTOPILOT_OK signal + platform-level duplicate suppression. Both are unnecessary: (1) TRIG-08 says 'always do something', making no-op signals contradictory. (2) At 4 pulses/day instead of 48, each pulse has ~6 hours of elapsed state changes, so 'nothing to report' and duplicate reminders are naturally rare. (3) Building message deduplication infrastructure is engineering effort with marginal v1 value. Dorabot runs every 30 min but is a general-purpose personal agent with many things to monitor. Sunder is a vertical CRM agent -- 4x/day is plenty. Fintool's proactive skill detector runs weekly. Prompt-level guidance ('don't repeat unless changed') is sufficient. Thread compaction handles context growth.

decisions.description: REVISED: No dedicated post-pulse write-back mechanism. Handled by existing infrastructure: (1) last_run_at / next_run_at updated automatically by cron scanner after every trigger execution (FOUND-02). (2) Task/CRM state changes already committed during the pulse via tool calls. (3) Pulse run record logged in runs table (TRIG-04). (4) Journal entry unnecessary — thread history provides continuity between pulses (Sunder fires into persistent thread, unlike Dorabot which uses fresh sessions). Only prompt-level instruction: 'after acting, summarize what you did and what's next in a concise message' — conversation hygiene, not a write-back system.
decisions.decision: No dedicated write-back. Existing infra (cron scanner, runs, thread history) covers all four original requirements.
decisions.reasoning: Original proposal had four write-back steps. All are already handled: (1) timestamps by cron scanner, (2) state changes by in-pulse tool calls, (3) run records by runs table, (4) journal by thread history. Dorabot needs explicit journal write-back because each pulse is a fresh session with zero conversation history — the journal is the only continuity. Sunder's autopilot fires into a persistent thread, so conversation history IS the continuity. Building a separate write-back system would duplicate what already exists. Tasklet similarly has no platform write-back — agent writes to files/DB during execution, not as a post-step. Prompt instruction for concise summary is sufficient.

decisions.description: REVISED (v3): Memory synthesis pulse — separate trigger from autopilot (different cadence, different purpose). V1 scope: weekly, one job — list memory files via read_file('/{clientId}/memory/') and check Storage metadata timestamps for files modified since last pulse. Read each changed file, surface a brief summary to the user ('here's what I wrote to memory this week, here's what seems stale, anything to correct?'). User replies in-thread to confirm/adjust. Agent updates or deletes memory files based on feedback via write_file. Also checks if MEMORY.md is approaching 200-line cap and self-organizes (moves detail to topic files, leaves pointers). Fires into its own dedicated thread (created alongside Autopilot thread on signup). If nothing changed since last pulse, stay silent (no-op). V2 DEFERRED: Growth Plan proposal/enrichment, Skill-Building Interview integration, performance review, pattern detection into named skill proposals, bi-directional feedback loop.
decisions.decision: REVISED: V1 weekly memory review using Storage metadata timestamps on memory files. Scoped to cleanup + summary. V2: Growth Plan integration, performance review, pattern detection.
decisions.reasoning: REVISED (v3) to align with MEM-03 v4 filesystem revert. Previous version queried agent_memories table with updated_at column — now uses Storage.list() with metadata to check last-modified timestamps on files under /{clientId}/memory/. Functionally equivalent: both answer 'what memory files changed since last pulse?' The agent reads each changed file, composes a summary, presents to user. Core purpose unchanged: safety net for MEM-05's aggressive auto-write policy. Fintool's model is the v1 reference: weekly, prompt-driven, 'if nothing stay silent.' V2 full spec preserved: (1) Pattern detection writing to memory/growth-plan.md with status:proposed. (2) Growth Plan enrichment. (3) Performance review. (4) User feedback auto-written back. (5) Configurable frequency.

decisions.description: REVISED: Two retry policies, not four. (1) Autopilot pulse: 0 retries — next pulse in ~6 hours is the natural retry. (2) All other triggers (user-created cron, webhooks): max 2 retries — cron scanner re-picks failed triggers on next tick (~1 min) if status='failed' and retry_count < 2. No exponential backoff needed (LLM calls, not rate-limited APIs). Deep-research and document-processing are not separate trigger types in v1. retry_count column on runs table, incremented on each re-pick. After max retries: status='failed_permanent', visible in UI, user can manually re-trigger.
decisions.decision: Two policies: autopilot 0 retries (next pulse is the retry), everything else max 2 retries via scanner re-pick.
decisions.reasoning: Original had four per-type retry configs with exponential backoff — overengineered for v1. No Temporal, no Trigger.dev, no retry orchestration engine. Cron scanner already ticks every minute (FOUND-02), so re-picking a failed trigger on the next tick is natural and free. Autopilot at ~4x/day doesn't need retries — the next pulse covers it, same as Dorabot and Tasklet (neither has retry infra). Deep-research and document-processing aren't separate trigger types in v1 — just two categories: autopilot and user-created. Exponential backoff solves API rate limiting, which isn't the failure mode here (LLM calls fail from timeouts or context errors, not 429s). 1-minute spacing via scanner tick is sufficient.

decisions.description: Tool-level idempotency as safety net (belt-and-suspenders with TRIG-02 atomic claim). External-facing tools include execution-scoped dedup to catch edge cases the claim mechanism misses (crashed function that re-executes, reaper that resets a claim too early). send_message tool: before sending, checks sent_messages table WHERE run_id = ? AND action = 'email' AND recipient = ? — if exists, skip. CRM insert tools: use INSERT...ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING where applicable. CRM update tools: inherently idempotent (UPDATE...SET is safe to replay). Internal tools (read_file, memory writes, task status updates): no dedup needed — idempotent by nature. Schema: sent_messages table (run_id, action, recipient, sent_at) for email dedup. This is the second layer — TRIG-02 claim prevents 99.9% of duplicates, this catches the rest.
decisions.decision: External-facing tools (send_message, CRM inserts) include execution-scoped idempotency checks. Internal tools are inherently idempotent. sent_messages table for email dedup.
decisions.reasoning: Completes the idempotency story started by TRIG-02 (atomic claim at scanner level). TRIG-02 prevents duplicate dispatch. TRIG-13 prevents duplicate side effects even if dispatch-level dedup fails. Three scenarios where claim can fail: (1) Vercel function crashes after starting work but before completing — reaper resets claim after 15 min, trigger re-fires, agent re-executes including already-sent emails. (2) Reaper runs too early (clock skew, slow function) — releases claim while original is still running. (3) Bug in claim logic. For all three, tool-level checks are the last line of defense. Tasklet dev recommended this explicitly: 'atomic update guards (WHERE already_sent IS NULL style).' Fintool has processed-pair tracking at the application layer for the same reason. Cost: one small table (sent_messages), one WHERE clause per send_message call, ON CONFLICT on CRM inserts. ~15 lines of code. The principle: platform-level dedup for dispatch, application-level dedup for side effects. Neither alone is sufficient; together they provide defense in depth.

decisions.description: REVISED: Thread identity — simple UUID-based threads. Schema: thread_id (UUID PK), client_id (FK), title (user-editable or auto-generated), is_pinned (for Autopilot thread), created_at, updated_at. Web UX is draft-first: /chat is a draft canvas, canonical thread is created lazily on first submitted message, then URL reconciles to /chat/{thread_id}. Added minimal channel foundation tables in v1: conversation_channel_mappings (channel + external_conversation_id -> thread_id) and conversation_channel_delivery_receipts (delivery idempotency). No chat_identity_key, no chat_type, no deterministic thread_key, no lane concept. Triggers reference threads by thread_id. Out-of-app notifications via email (TRIG-06b). V2: Telegram channel added via Vercel AI SDK Chat SDK adapter. V3: WhatsApp via Meta Cloud API.
decisions.decision: Simple UUID threads with lazy server-side creation on first message. Keep lightweight channel mapping + delivery idempotency tables in v1 foundation.
decisions.reasoning: Draft-first avoids empty thread spam and matches Chat SDK interaction flow. Canonical thread identity remains UUID-based in conversation_threads. The mapping + receipt tables are intentionally minimal and channel-agnostic: they provide stable external-conversation -> thread resolution and retry-safe delivery dedup without introducing full multi-channel routing complexity. This keeps v1 web UX clean while making Telegram/WhatsApp adapters a thin integration layer in v2/v3.

decisions.removed_reason: Conversation forking / lane-based routing removed. Lanes don't exist in web-only v1. Multi-channel thread routing deferred to v2/v3 per SESSION-01.
Duplicate of TRIG-06 (per-thread concurrency model). Concurrency serialization is a trigger/scheduling concern, not a session concern.

decisions.description: REVISED: No pause-and-resume checkpoint system. Agent asks a question, run completes, lock released. User replies whenever. Reply triggers a new run with full thread history — LLM sees its own question and the user's answer, continues naturally. Thread history IS the checkpoint. Same model as Tasklet (no checkpoint, conversation context is continuity). No waiting_user_input status, no resume logic, no persisted checkpoints.
decisions.decision: No checkpoint/resume. Run completes after asking a question. Thread history provides continuity for the next run.
decisions.reasoning: Blueprint proposed explicit state machine (waiting_user_input status, persisted checkpoint, lock release/reacquire). Unnecessary given persistent thread history — the LLM can re-read what it was doing and pick up, same as Tasklet. Dorabot blocks the run (AskUserQuestion sleeps waiting), which ties up resources. Fintool has no pause concept. Tasklet's model is simplest and proven: run completes, user replies, new run sees full history. The only tradeoff: agent can't resume mid-execution-step (e.g., halfway through a multi-tool-call sequence). In practice, the agent re-derives intent from thread history and continues, which is the same pattern every Tasklet trigger run uses.

decisions.removed_reason: Approval checkpoint system deemed unnecessary — same reasoning as SESSION-04 (no pause-and-resume). Agent asks for approval in-thread, run completes, user replies to approve/reject, next run acts accordingly. No checkpoint infrastructure needed.

Custom replay infrastructure unnecessary. Vercel AI SDK handles streaming replay natively. Thread history in conversation_messages table is the replay log — no separate replay persistence layer needed.

decisions.description: Long-thread compaction — when thread exceeds context budget, summarize older messages, keep recent turns verbatim. Never delete source history in thread_messages table. Prompt assembly order: system prompt (identity, tools, instructions) → memory (client profile, preferences) → compaction summary (older messages summarized) → recent turns verbatim → current message/trigger event. IMPLEMENTATION: Use provider-native compaction via AI SDK provider options (e.g., Anthropic compact_20260112 with token threshold trigger + CRM-tuned instructions: 'preserve deal names, contact details, task statuses, decisions made'). Implementation note: Sunder uses mixed models via AI SDK Gateway/OpenRouter (per LLM routing decisions). Verify compaction support per provider — Anthropic has native compact_20260112, other providers may need prepareStep-based DIY compaction as fallback. Fallback: AI SDK prepareStep callback for providers without native compaction (summarize old messages, send only recent turns). V1: start with provider-native, add prepareStep fallback if needed.
decisions.decision: Provider-native compaction first, prepareStep fallback for providers without it. Source history never deleted.
decisions.reasoning: AI SDK has no built-in compaction, but Anthropic's API has native compact_20260112 supported via AI SDK provider options — zero custom code, server-side. Tasklet builds custom compaction ('LLM-based compactions, hide tool results, sub-agents get tailored portions'). Dorabot sidesteps it with fresh sessions per pulse. Fintool has no compaction (short-lived alert runs). For Sunder, threads are persistent and accumulate (especially Autopilot at 4x/day), so compaction is essential. Native provider compaction is the lowest-effort v1 path. CRM-tuned compaction instructions ensure deal names, contact details, and task statuses survive summarization. prepareStep fallback available if non-Anthropic models lack native compaction. Source messages in thread_messages table are never deleted — compaction is read-time prompt assembly, not destructive.

decisions.description: REVISED (v2): Assistant personality — /{clientId}/SOUL.md file in Supabase Storage. Runner reads via Storage API and loads into system prompt position #1 every run. Default SOUL.md seeded on signup. User-editable from settings UI (frontend reads/writes via Storage API). Agent cannot auto-edit (read_file allowed, write_file blocked on SOUL.md path by runner). Same concept as Dorabot's SOUL.md, now stored as actual file — aligning with Tasklet/Fintool filesystem-first pattern.
decisions.decision: REVERTED from DB column to filesystem. /{clientId}/SOUL.md in Supabase Storage. Agent read-only, user-editable from settings.
decisions.reasoning: Original v1 stored personality as DB column, reasoning 'web SaaS not local app.' Reverted because: (1) DATA-04 storage layout already defined SOUL.md as a file — DB column created internal contradiction, (2) RUNNER-03 context assembly already references 'SOUL.md personality' as first layer — was always designed as a file, (3) both Tasklet and Fintool store agent instructions/personality as files, (4) Supabase Storage is already in the stack for skills/subagents/artifacts — personality is the same kind of content, (5) settings UI reads/writes Storage directly via supabase.storage.from('agent-files').download/upload — no harder than a DB column update. Key constraints unchanged: agent cannot auto-edit (runner blocks write_file to SOUL.md path), default seeded on signup, loaded every run.

decisions.description: REVISED (v2): User profile — /{clientId}/USER.md file in Supabase Storage. Agent can read and write via read_file/write_file tools. User can review/edit from settings UI. Runner reads via Storage API and loads into system prompt position #2 every run. Populated organically as agent learns about the user during conversations and autopilot pulses (TRIG-08 priority #8: 'get to know the user, ask one concise question per pulse if profile is sparse'). Deep Profile Interview deferred to v2. Empty/default USER.md seeded on signup — agent fills it over time.
decisions.decision: REVERTED from DB column to filesystem. /{clientId}/USER.md in Supabase Storage. Agent read+write via read_file/write_file. Organic population.
decisions.reasoning: Same revert rationale as MEM-01. DATA-04 already defined USER.md in storage layout. RUNNER-03 already references it as position #2 in context assembly. Agent writes profile updates via the same write_file tool it uses for everything else — no separate tool needed. Markdown format is more natural for the LLM than structured JSON (agent writes 'Name: Seth\nTimezone: SGT\nPrefers: concise CMAs, text over calls' rather than constructing JSON). Settings UI reads/writes Storage directly. Fintool's UserMemories.md is the direct precedent — markdown file the user controls, agent sees every conversation.

decisions.description: REVISED (v4): Auto memory system — filesystem-first, following both Tasklet and Fintool patterns. Three file layers in Supabase Storage: (1) /{clientId}/SOUL.md — personality, loaded every run, agent read-only (MEM-01), (2) /{clientId}/USER.md — profile, loaded every run, agent read+write (MEM-02), (3) /{clientId}/MEMORY.md + /{clientId}/memory/*.md — auto memory files. Agent interacts via the same read_file/write_file tools used for everything else. No separate memory_read/memory_write tools. No agent_memories DB table. MEMORY.md is a WORKING NOTEBOOK — agent writes memories directly into it by default. As it grows toward the 200-line cap, agent moves detailed content to topic files (/{clientId}/memory/preferences.md, etc.) and leaves pointers behind. First 200 lines of MEMORY.md loaded into system prompt position #3 every run. Topic files read on-demand via read_file. Four seeded files on signup: memory/preferences.md, memory/growth-plan.md, memory/patterns.md, memory/key-decisions.md (empty, ready for use). Agent creates additional files as needed via write_file — taxonomy is seeded, not locked. Discovery: agent calls read_file('/{clientId}/memory/') to see all topic files. System-reminder advertises memory state (file count, last-modified timestamps from Storage metadata) so agent loads specific files on-demand. No CRM duplication — memory holds ONLY what the CRM schema cannot capture.
decisions.decision: REVERTED from DB table to filesystem. /{clientId}/MEMORY.md + /{clientId}/memory/*.md in Supabase Storage. Same read_file/write_file tools. agent_memories table dropped.
decisions.reasoning: REVISED (v4) reverting DB-first back to filesystem-first. Key reasons: (1) DATA-04 storage layout already defined MEMORY.md and memory/ directory — DB table created internal contradiction, (2) agent was already 'thinking in files' via memory_read/memory_write tool names that mimicked filesystem ops — the abstraction leaked, just make it real files, (3) one tool surface (read_file/write_file) for memory + subagents + skills + artifacts is simpler than maintaining two parallel tool surfaces, (4) Tasklet stores memory in /agent/home/ files, Fintool stores memory in /private/memories/UserMemories.md — both production-proven filesystem patterns, (5) Fintool's Nicolas: 'S3 for files is a better database than a database' — durability, human-readability, simplicity. Trade-offs accepted: (a) no Postgres transactions — last-write-wins on concurrent writes, acceptable because memory writes are seconds apart and synthesis pulse catches conflicts weekly, (b) no updated_at column — Storage metadata provides last-modified timestamps, synthesis pulse can query Storage.list() with metadata, (c) no ILIKE search across memory content — agent reads MEMORY.md index to find the right topic file, same as Claude Code pattern. The DB approach was solving problems we don't have at our scale (<50 clients, <20 memory files per client).

decisions.description: REVISED (v3): Memory taxonomy — four seeded files in /{clientId}/memory/ + agent freedom to create more. Seeded on signup: (1) memory/preferences.md — working style, communication prefs, tool preferences. Written when agent detects lasting preference ('never cold-call sellers', 'prefers concise CMAs'). Each entry timestamped inline. (2) memory/growth-plan.md — skill-building roadmap. Populated organically as agent detects patterns (v1) or via Deep Profile Interview (v2). Entries have status: proposed/approved/in-progress/built. (3) memory/patterns.md — recurring behaviors with evidence dates. Agent writes after 3+ instances of same behavior. Confirmed patterns may promote to growth-plan items. (4) memory/key-decisions.md — umbrella file for small decisions. For significant decisions, agent creates a dedicated file like memory/key-decisions/2026-02-26-123-main-pricing.md. Never deleted — institutional memory. Agent creates additional files as needed via write_file — follows Claude Code pattern where topic organization is emergent, not locked. Dropped from original: daily-changelog (KISS — synthesis pulse checks Storage metadata timestamps), context files (CRM data, not memory).
decisions.decision: REVERTED to filesystem language. Four seeded .md files in /{clientId}/memory/ + organic extension via write_file.
decisions.reasoning: REVISED (v3) restoring filesystem language now that MEM-03 v4 reverted to Storage. 'memory/preferences.md' is a real file path again, not a DB file_key pretending to be one. Key-decisions can use actual subdirectories (memory/key-decisions/topic.md) since Storage supports path prefixes natively. Agent discovers all memory files via read_file('/{clientId}/memory/') — returns tree listing. Same four categories, same write conditions, same boundary rules. Just real files instead of DB rows.

decisions.description: REVISED (v3): Auto-write memory during runs — agent reads and writes memory files during any run (chat or trigger) using the same read_file/write_file tools used for everything else. No separate memory tools. No approval gate. Default write target is /{clientId}/MEMORY.md (working notebook). If a memory clearly fits a seeded category, agent writes to that topic file directly via write_file. Write conditions by file: (1) memory/preferences.md — write immediately when user states a lasting preference explicitly or implicitly ('just text her, I never cold-call'). Do NOT write transient requests ('send it now'). (2) memory/patterns.md — write after 3+ instances of same behavior. Include evidence dates. (3) memory/key-decisions/[topic].md — write when a significant, hard-to-reverse decision is made. Include reasoning. Creates a new file per major decision. (4) memory/growth-plan.md — write when synthesis pulse promotes a confirmed pattern, or user explicitly requests. (5) New files — agent creates via write_file('/{clientId}/memory/new-topic.md', content) when observation doesn't fit existing files. What NOT to save (follows Claude Code): session-specific context, information already in CRM database, speculative or unverified conclusions from a single instance. MEMORY.md is a working notebook — agent writes directly into it. As it grows toward the 200-line cap, agent moves detailed content to topic files and leaves pointers. Personality (MEM-01) is agent read-only via read_file. User profile (MEM-02) is agent read+write via read_file/write_file. Synthesis pulse (TRIG-11) is the cleanup safety net — reviews memory files weekly.
decisions.decision: REVERTED to filesystem tools. Auto-write during runs via read_file/write_file on /{clientId}/memory/ paths. No separate memory tools. Same write conditions + weekly synthesis pulse.
decisions.reasoning: REVISED (v3) aligning with MEM-03 v4 filesystem revert. Key simplification: memory_read/memory_write tools eliminated. Agent uses the same read_file/write_file it already has for subagents, skills, and artifacts. One tool surface for all file operations. The LLM doesn't need to remember which tool to use for memory vs other files — it's always read_file/write_file. Per-file write conditions unchanged. Synthesis pulse now queries Storage.list('/{clientId}/memory/') with metadata to check last-modified timestamps instead of SQL updated_at — functionally equivalent.

decisions.description: REVISED (v3): Memory shared across all threads for same client — all threads for same client_id read/write the same files under /{clientId}/MEMORY.md and /{clientId}/memory/. Thread A writes to memory/preferences.md, Thread B reads it next run. Autopilot thread reads all memory files. No private-thread memory mode in v1. Concurrent write risk: last-write-wins on Supabase Storage (no Postgres transactions for files). Acceptable because memory writes are sparse (seconds apart in practice) and synthesis pulse catches conflicts weekly.
decisions.decision: REVERTED to filesystem. Shared memory via /{clientId}/ Storage path prefix. Last-write-wins accepted.
decisions.reasoning: Claude Code pattern: per-project memory directory shared across all sessions. Sunder maps project→client_id, session→thread. Same pattern. Memory is the bridge between threads — Thread A learns 'seller prefers text', Thread B uses that knowledge next morning without the user repeating it. Concurrent write risk is now last-write-wins (no Postgres row locking for Storage files). This is acceptable: (1) memory writes happen during agent runs which are serialized per thread (one run per thread at a time per TRIG-06), (2) cross-thread concurrent writes would require two threads writing to the same memory file at the exact same second — extremely unlikely with <50 clients, (3) synthesis pulse reviews all memory files weekly and catches any inconsistencies, (4) Fintool accepts the same trade-off with S3 files. If concurrent writes become a real problem at scale, add a lightweight mutex (advisory lock in Postgres before Storage write) — but don't build it until it's needed.

decisions.description: Memory as primary switching cost/moat — compounding value curve from Week 1 (basic prefs) to Month 6+ (pattern detection, growth plan skills). Cannot be replicated by competitors. Memory files are human-readable markdown — user can view and edit from settings UI (v2). Export/portability deferred.
decisions.decision: Memory as moat. Compounding personalization is the defensibility layer.
decisions.reasoning: Every session makes the agent better. Week 1: knows your name and timezone. Month 1: knows you prefer text over calls, always want concise CMAs, focus on District 9. Month 3: detects you do CMAs 3x/month and proposes auto-CMA skill. Month 6: patterns.md has 20+ entries, growth-plan has 5 built skills, key-decisions has institutional memory a new assistant couldn't replicate. Switching cost compounds — a competitor starts from zero. Claude Code validates the pattern at scale: developers don't switch away because their MEMORY.md and CLAUDE.md encode months of project knowledge. Same dynamic, different domain.

decisions.description: REVISED: Mixed autonomy (Option B) — two tiers. Auto-run for internal work (CRM reads, memory writes, drafts, summaries, research, internal tasks, web search, knowledge base reads). Approval-required for external-facing work (outbound comms, client-facing CRM changes, calendar scheduling, deletions, publishing artifacts). One rule: if it leaves the system or is visible to someone other than the user, it needs approval. Internal work is autonomous, external-facing work pauses.
decisions.decision: Mixed autonomy. Internal = auto, external-facing = approval required.
decisions.reasoning: Three options evaluated: (A) Full autonomy (Tasklet-lean) — agent runs all tools without pausing. Too risky for CRM that touches real clients. One bad email is unrecoverable. (B) Mixed autonomy (Sunder blueprint / Fintool-lean) — two tiers based on blast radius. Agent is useful autonomously for most work, user only interrupted for things that matter. (C) Approve everything — notification fatigue, defeats autonomy value prop, would gate memory writes which kills Claude Code pattern (MEM-05). Option B selected. Tasklet has no explicit approval mechanism — trusts system prompt. Fintool pauses background agents at 'needs confirmation' actions. Sunder follows Fintool pattern with a simpler rule: internal vs external-facing.

decisions.description: REVISED: Approval gate is a chat interaction, not a special UI. Agent hits a high-risk tool call → instead of executing, presents the action in plain language in the thread ('I drafted this email to Mrs. Tan. Want me to send it?') → waits for user reply. User says yes → agent continues execution. User says no → run ends as cancelled. No special approval UI, no modal, no notification system. Just a conversation turn. Works identically in chat and trigger threads (trigger thread shows the pending action, user opens thread to respond).
decisions.decision: Approval gate via natural language in thread. No special approval UI.
decisions.reasoning: Simplest possible implementation. The agent already talks to the user in threads — asking permission is just another message. No approval queue, no notification badge, no modal dialog. Fintool does the same: 'confirmation appears in the conversation, user responds to continue.' Tasklet has no approval mechanism. For trigger threads: the pending action sits in the thread until the user opens it. Combined with TRIG-06b (email notification), the platform can email the user 'Sunder needs your approval in [thread name]' to prevent stale pending actions. Rejection ends the run — agent doesn't retry or rephrase, it accepts the no.

decisions.description: REVISED: Required safeguards — slimmed to what v1 actually needs. (1) Audit trail: every tool call and result is a thread_message — comes free from thread history. (2) Memory auto-writes exempt from approval gates (MEM-05). (3) Personality is agent read-only (MEM-01). (4) One run per thread (TRIG-06). Everything else from original (idempotency/dedupe, preflight checks, structured subagent outputs, replay persistence, versioned memory rollback) is YAGNI for v1. Synthesis pulse (TRIG-11) is the memory correction mechanism, not versioned rollback.
decisions.decision: V1 safeguards: audit trail via thread history + memory exempt from approval + personality read-only + per-thread serialization. Everything else deferred.
decisions.reasoning: Original SAFETY-03 was a kitchen sink. Half already covered by other decisions (MEM-01, MEM-05, TRIG-06), half is YAGNI. Idempotency/dedupe: what would be duplicated? User messages are unique, trigger fires are serialized per-thread. Preflight checks: vague — just 'write good prompts.' Versioned memory rollback: over-engineering — synthesis pulse is the correction mechanism, agent rewrites the row. Replay persistence: thread history IS the replay log. Stripped to four concrete safeguards that are either already built or trivially free.

decisions.description: REVISED: Approval matrix — simple two-column rule. AUTO-RUN: CRM reads (search contacts, view deals), memory reads/writes, generate drafts (CMA, email, notes), web search/research, create/update internal tasks, write to thread (responses, summaries), read knowledge base. APPROVAL REQUIRED: send email, send Telegram (v2), send WhatsApp/SMS (v3), create/update client-facing CRM records, schedule meetings on user's calendar, delete anything, publish artifacts (share links externally). Rule: if it leaves the system or is visible to someone other than the user, approval required. No per-action granularity, no configurable matrix in v1. One rule, two columns.
decisions.decision: Two-column approval matrix. Internal = auto, external-facing = approval. Not configurable in v1.
decisions.reasoning: Resolves the 'NOT finalized' status from V1 spec. The matrix is intentionally simple — one rule (internal vs external-facing) rather than per-action configuration. Configurable approval levels (e.g., 'always auto-approve emails to this contact') are a v2 trust-building feature. V1 errs on the side of caution for all external-facing actions. The boundary is clear: CRM reads are internal (no one sees them), CRM writes that affect client-facing data need approval (wrong data visible to clients), emails/messages leave the system entirely (highest risk). Memory writes are explicitly exempt — memory IS the product value (MEM-05), gating it would cripple the Claude Code auto-memory pattern.

decisions.description: REVISED: Four connection types — follows Tasklet pattern exactly (ref: tasklet/tools/00-complete-tasklet-tool-definitions-verbatim.md). (1) integrations — pre-built OAuth connections via Composio (Sunder's equivalent of Tasklet's static + Pipedream backend). Handles OAuth, token refresh, pre-built actions. (2) mcp — custom MCP server connections. Agent or user provides HTTPS endpoint. (3) direct_api — raw HTTP API with explicit auth config (bearer, header, basic, query-parameter, custom-oauth). Agent researches API and sets up connection; user just approves auth. (4) computer_use — remote browser automation. Provider decision (Browserbase vs Firecrawl vs Vercel Agent Browser) deferred to SERVICE-12. Selection order follows Tasklet: reuse existing → integrations → mcp → direct_api → computer_use. Agent manages all connection setup — user only approves auth flows and tool activation.
decisions.decision: Four connection types: integrations (Composio), mcp, direct_api, computer_use. Follows Tasklet taxonomy exactly. computer_use is defined in the v1 taxonomy for completeness but returns 'not yet available' until SERVICE-12 ships in v2.
decisions.reasoning: Tasklet has four connection types in production: integrations (static + Pipedream), mcp, direct_api, computer_use. Sunder adopts the same taxonomy with Composio replacing Tasklet's static + Pipedream integration backend. Key insight from Tasklet research: MCP and direct_api are agent-managed, not user-managed. The agent discovers it needs an API, creates the connection, and the user just approves auth / pastes an API key. This means even non-technical users (real estate agents) benefit from direct_api — they don't configure anything, they just approve. Computer_use maps to Tasklet's persistent VM concept — specific provider (Browserbase for persistent auth, Firecrawl for stateless, or Vercel Agent Browser) is a SERVICE-12 decision, deferred to v2. In v1 the connection type exists in the enum but the agent will not attempt it — the selection chain stops at direct_api. Fintool has no connection model — everything is hand-built for finance. Original Sunder blueprint had three types (no computer_use); adding it follows Tasklet and enables browser automation use cases (MLS scraping, property portal checks) when SERVICE-12 ships.

decisions.description: REVISED: Composio as integration backend — Sunder's equivalent of Tasklet's static + Pipedream backend. Handles OAuth flows, token refresh, pre-built actions. Agent calls Composio actions, not raw APIs. Composio covers the 'integrations' connection type from CONN-01. For the long tail not covered by Composio, agent falls through to mcp → direct_api → computer_use (CONN-01 selection order).
decisions.decision: Composio as integration backend for OAuth connections. Fallback to other connection types for uncovered services.
decisions.reasoning: Tasklet builds top ~30 integrations in-house, proxies 3000+ through Pipedream. Sunder doesn't have the resources to build in-house integrations — Composio is the equivalent third-party marketplace. Same role: handle OAuth complexity so we don't build token refresh, scope management, etc. Agent interface is the same: discover → create → activate tools → use. Fintool has no marketplace (hand-built). Composio's 141+ pre-built actions cover the v1 services (Gmail, Google Calendar, Cal.com, Tally.so). For anything Composio doesn't cover, the Tasklet-pattern fallback chain (mcp → direct_api → computer_use) handles it.
decisions.future_evaluation: CONN-04 tracks googleworkspace/cli as a potential Composio replacement for Google services. Re-evaluate when it reaches v1.0.

decisions.description: Connection-first setup behavior — follows Tasklet system prompt instruction verbatim: 'ALWAYS prefer to use existing connections over creating new connections if the existing connection will work. You MUST use list_users_connections to check the users' existing connections first before creating new connections.' One-shot setup: user approves OAuth/auth once, connection persists, agent reuses automatically on subsequent runs. Agent discovers → checks existing → creates only if needed → activates tools → uses.
decisions.decision: Reuse-first, one-shot setup. Tasklet pattern verbatim.
decisions.reasoning: Direct Tasklet alignment. The system prompt instruction prevents connection sprawl (agent creating duplicate Gmail connections) and reduces user friction (approve once, never again). Fintool has no connection concept. The lifecycle: agent needs Gmail → calls list_users_connections → finds existing gmail connection → uses it. No re-auth, no re-approval. If no connection exists: agent calls create_new_connections → user approves OAuth → connection persists → future runs reuse. Token refresh handled by Composio (CONN-02) transparently.

decisions.description: EVALUATION (2026-03-05): googleworkspace/cli (github.com/googleworkspace/cli) as potential dedicated Google Workspace integration, replacing Composio for Google services (Drive, Gmail, Calendar, Sheets, Docs). The CLI provides a built-in MCP server (`gws mcp -s drive,gmail,calendar`) that exposes Google APIs as structured tools via MCP over stdio. Dynamically generates tool surface from Google's Discovery Service — auto-updated, complete API coverage. Free, open-source (Apache-2.0). Supports service accounts, OAuth, headless/CI credentials, pre-obtained access tokens.
decisions.decision: NOT APPROVED for v1. Re-evaluate when googleworkspace/cli reaches v1.0. Keep Composio (CONN-02) as integration backend.
decisions.reasoning: Evaluated 2026-03-05. The CLI is v0.3.4, first published 2026-03-03 (2 days before evaluation). 9 releases in 2 days — extremely rapid, unstable iteration. Explicit 'expect breaking changes before v1.0' warning. Carries 'not an officially supported Google product' disclaimer despite being in the googleworkspace GitHub org. Critical gaps for Sunder's multi-tenant SaaS: (1) No per-user OAuth token management — designed for single-user/service-account, would require building entire token lifecycle (storage, encryption, refresh, isolation) ourselves; (2) CLI-first architecture, not a TypeScript library — integrating into Next.js API routes requires subprocess/sidecar bridging; (3) No triggers/webhooks for reactive workflows. However, compelling long-term properties: zero per-action cost (vs Composio pricing at scale), complete auto-updated API coverage, MCP-native design aligns with CONN-01 taxonomy, full token custody control (no third-party storage). If it reaches v1.0 with documented multi-user auth patterns, it could replace Composio specifically for Google services while keeping Composio for non-Google integrations (Cal.com, Tally.so, etc.).
watch_triggers: Composio costs exceed $200/mo for Google actions

decisions.description: CRM: Custom on Supabase (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Core to architecture. Custom CRM tables in Supabase — full control, no per-seat fees, AI reads/writes directly via SQL, RLS for multi-tenant isolation.

decisions.description: Knowledge Base: Custom (Supabase Storage + Supabase DB metadata index) (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Supabase Storage for blobs + metadata table in Supabase DB for structured discovery. No sync layer (write to both at upload time). No vector/RAG — context window is the database. Metadata table included from day one to avoid folder-scanning as file count grows. Simpler than Fintool S3+Lambda sync, more discoverable than Tasklet bare filesystem.

decisions.description: Web search: Brave Search API LLM Context (default) + Exa /contents text mode (known URLs) + Parallel Search (optional A/B) (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Ship Brave Search API (LLM Context mode) as default general-purpose search + Exa /contents for URL content extraction. Both day-one — Exa costs nothing until used and "read this URL" is a core use case. Parallel Search kept as optional dev tooling for A/B testing providers, not user-facing. Horizontal product cannot justify vertical search pipelines like Fintool.

decisions.description: Scheduling: Cal.com via Composio (user's free account) (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Standard integration. Users connect own free Cal.com account via Composio. No calendar infrastructure to maintain.

decisions.description: Forms: Tally.so via Composio (user's free account) (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Standard integration. Users connect own Tally.so account via Composio. No form infrastructure to maintain.

decisions.description: Voice input: OpenAI Whisper (speech-to-text) (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to v2. Primary interface is web chat (SESSION-01). Voice input not needed for MVP.

decisions.description: Voice output: Inworld AI (cloned voice for user→client notes, AI voice for AI→user replies) (MVP basic)
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to v2. Voice output not needed when primary interface is web chat (SESSION-01). Revisit alongside SERVICE-06.

decisions.description: Meeting transcripts: Granola (user-managed, optional, MCP connection) (MVP ad hoc)
decisions.reasoning: Optional user-managed MCP connection. No platform dependency — user connects their own Granola account. AI pulls transcripts for post-meeting follow-ups. Zero cost to us if unused.

decisions.description: Document extraction: Two lanes — Lane 1 (comprehension): LLM reads document directly, no pipeline. Lane 2 (structured extraction): Existing Sunder pipeline (Gemini Flash split/classify + ExtendAI structured extraction + validation + human review). Option C routing: always start Lane 1, escalate to Lane 2 on request or when pre-configured schema exists. (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Leverages existing Sunder extraction pipeline (Gemini Flash + ExtendAI + OCR confidence + citations + business rule validation). Two lanes: comprehension (cheap, LLM-direct) vs structured extraction (full pipeline). Option C routing — Lane 1 by default, AI suggests Lane 2 when schema exists. Cheap by default, structured when needed. Avoids wasting ExtendAI cost on simple read queries.

decisions.description: Document generation: Vercel Sandbox + custom skills with appropriate libraries (xlsx, pdf-lib, docx, etc.). No separate service — AI writes code in sandbox to generate documents. Skills customized per output format. (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: No dedicated doc-gen service needed. Sandbox is the document generator — AI writes code using libraries (xlsx, pdf-lib, etc.) via custom skills. Reference: github.com/anthropics/skills/tree/main/skills/xlsx. Simpler than a custom MCP, more flexible (any format the library supports), zero additional infrastructure.

decisions.description: Artifact publishing (Mini Lovable): DEFERRED to v2. AI can generate artifacts in sandbox but shareable URL hosting not needed for MVP.
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to v2. Artifact generation still works (sandbox produces files), but publishing/hosting adds infrastructure for a feature that is not core to v1 value prop. Revisit when client-facing sharing is a real need.

decisions.description: Interactive browser (Lane 3): Stagehand + Browserbase — action caching for ~80% speedup on repeated actions, persistent browser contexts for login-gated sites (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to v3. Interactive browser (Stagehand + Browserbase) is expensive and complex. Not needed for v1/v2. Requires Trigger.dev for async execution (Trigger.dev deferred to v3 per FOUND-02).

decisions.description: Known-site scraping (Lane 1): Apify actors + RapidAPI — pre-built maintained scrapers for PropertyGuru, LinkedIn bulk, directories. Per-run pricing (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to v3. Known-site scraping (Apify + RapidAPI) requires Trigger.dev for async crawls (Trigger.dev deferred to v3 per FOUND-02). Revisit when Trigger.dev ships.

decisions.description: Open-ended scraping (Lane 2): Scrapling (BSD-3, Python) + MiniMax M2.5 (~$20-50/mo flat-rate) — general-purpose 'point at any site' scraping. Runs as async background job in Vercel Sandbox via Trigger.dev. User prompts, comes back when done (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to v3. Open-ended scraping requires Trigger.dev (Trigger.dev deferred to v3 per FOUND-02). Exa (SERVICE-03) covers basic URL content extraction for now.

decisions.description: LinkedIn intelligence: LinkedIn MCP server (Apache 2.0, read-only, Patchright browser, 7 tools) for interactive lookups + RapidAPI LinkedIn for bulk scraping. ToS risk acknowledged — rate limiting and compliance controls required (MVP)
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to v2. LinkedIn intelligence (MCP + RapidAPI bulk) not needed for MVP. Single lookups could be added easily later — bulk enrichment requires Trigger.dev.

decisions.description: Email: Resend — DEFERRED to Phase 3
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to Phase 3. Transactional/outbound email via Resend. Needs core chat + document workflows first.

decisions.description: Social media: Postiz — DEFERRED to Phase 2
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to Phase 2. Social media scheduling via Postiz (open-source). Needs core product first.

decisions.description: Link attribution: Short.io via Composio — DEFERRED to Phase 3
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to Phase 3. Branded short links via Short.io + Composio. Needs email and social to exist first.

decisions.description: Document signing: DocuSeal — DEFERRED to Phase 3+
decisions.reasoning: Deferred to Phase 3+. E-signatures via DocuSeal (open-source). Needs document generation and deal workflows first.

decisions.description: Chat is home screen. Mission Control is a control surface, not primary workspace.
decisions.reasoning: Core UX principle. Chat is the primary workspace — users live here. Mission Control is a check-in surface, not a daily driver.

decisions.description: Primary navigation: AGENT (Chat, Mission Control, Tasks, Automations, Memory) / DATABASE (CRM, Knowledge, Documents, Channels) / SYSTEM (Settings)
decisions.reasoning: Clean three-group nav: AGENT (AI actions) / DATABASE (data) / SYSTEM (config). Separates what the AI does from what it works with.

decisions.description: Mission Control: two tabs only — Overview (status dashboard) and Queue (approvals, failures, blocked tasks, goals at risk)
decisions.reasoning: Two tabs only — Overview (status dashboard) and Queue (actionable items). Keeps Mission Control focused. Not a project management tool.

decisions.description: Tasks: unified surface merging CRM tasks + Agent Tasks. Board (default), List, Goals views. Columns: Planned, In Progress, Review, Done. Blocked/Needs approval are badges, not columns.
decisions.reasoning: Unified surface. CRM tasks and agent tasks on one board — the AI is a teammate, its work belongs alongside yours. Task type (CRM vs agent) is a filter/badge, not a separate page. Board default (Planned → In Progress → Review → Done), Blocked/Needs Approval as badges not columns. List and Goals as alternate views.

decisions.description: Structured AI input primitive — AI can present clickable options with optional freeform input. AI decides when and what options. V1: web buttons via streamUI. V2: Telegram inline keyboards. V3: WhatsApp list/button messages. Not a dedicated interview engine — a conversation pattern. Onboarding, skill-building, workflow creation are skills that use this primitive.
decisions.reasoning: Simplified from "Guided Interview UI engine" to a UI primitive. The AI presents structured choices when useful — clickable options + freeform escape hatch. V1: web app buttons via streamUI. V2: Telegram inline keyboards via Vercel AI SDK Chat SDK. V3: WhatsApp native list/button messages. No dedicated engine needed.

decisions.description: Session rail in left sidebar — clicking Chat opens a draft canvas, thread is created server-side on first submitted message, one active chat canvas at a time, no duplicate thread navigator inside chat canvas
decisions.reasoning: Aligns with Vercel Chat SDK style and avoids pre-created empty threads. Chat nav opens a draft state, first submit calls /api/chat, server resolves/creates canonical thread_id, and client reconciles URL once canonical ID is known. One active canvas, no split views/tabs.

decisions.description: Channels page — v1: coming-soon placeholder. v2: Telegram (via Vercel AI SDK Chat SDK). v3: WhatsApp (via Meta Cloud API). Channel page shows connection status, test send, delivery diagnostics.
decisions.reasoning: v1 placeholder. No implementation needed. v2: Telegram via Vercel AI SDK Chat SDK adapter (https://chat-sdk.dev/docs/adapters/telegram). v3: WhatsApp via Meta Cloud API.

decisions.description: Frontend inspirations — CRM: Twenty. Knowledge: Outline. Tasks: Super Productivity. Interview cards: Pearl + Fintool AskUserQuestion.
decisions.reasoning: Reference list for UI design phase. Twenty (CRM), Outline (knowledge), Super Productivity (tasks), Pearl + Fintool (interview cards). Not an architecture decision — design inspiration.

decisions.description: Two-phase onboarding — Phase 1 Quick Activation (<10 min): sign up, connect services, provision runtime, first useful output. Phase 2 Deep Profile Interview (scheduled within first week, 30-60 min): structured intake interview via Guided Interview UI with branching by role/domain, actively populates USER.md + preferences.md, proposes Growth Plan (memory/growth-plan.md). Phase 2 not required for activation but required for full personalization. If skipped, falls back to passive observation and autopilot pulse re-suggests (max once per 48h).
decisions.reasoning: Two-phase onboarding. Phase 1 (<10 min): sign up, connect services, first useful output — get to value fast. Phase 2 (first week): AI asks structured questions in normal chat using UX-05 primitive — populates USER.md + preferences.md, proposes Growth Plan. Not required for activation. Falls back to passive observation if skipped. Re-suggests max once per 48h.

decisions.description: Tasklet patterns as default behavior — if spec is silent, follow Tasklet references. Any deviation must be documented in Delta Register.
decisions.reasoning: Governance rule. Tasklet patterns are the default — deviations documented in Delta Register. Keeps architecture decisions traceable.

decisions.description: 16 documented deltas from Tasklet (DELTA-001 through DELTA-016) — all require acknowledgment as accepted divergences
decisions.reasoning: 16 deltas (DELTA-001 through DELTA-016) acknowledged as intentional divergences. Each was approved in its respective category (infra, data, execution, etc.).

decisions.description: Areas kept close to Tasklet (no planned delta): stateless invocation model, subagent execution pattern, trigger fresh-invocation semantics, context truncation recovery, determinism hardening
decisions.reasoning: Confirmed alignment with Tasklet on: stateless invocation, subagent execution, trigger semantics, context truncation recovery, determinism hardening. No planned deltas.

decisions.description: Context recovery contract — on <context-removed>, recover from /agent/toolcalls/{blockId}/result before using data. If recovery fails and safe re-run possible, re-run. Otherwise return partial and ask for guidance.
decisions.reasoning: Standard resilience pattern from Tasklet. On context truncation: recover from saved toolcall results → re-run if safe → return partial and ask user. Protects long conversations.

decisions.description: Dual task model — CRM tasks (tracking-only, binary open/completed) + Agent Tasks (executable, status lifecycle planning→planned→in_progress→review→done/cancelled). Unified Tasks page in UI.
decisions.reasoning: Dual task model: CRM tasks (tracking-only, open/completed) + Agent tasks (executable, full lifecycle). Both surface on unified Tasks page (UX-04). Matches Tasklet pattern with CRM extension.

decisions.description: REVISED per FOUND-02: v1 (0-50 clients): current architecture unchanged. Fixed costs ~$45/mo (Vercel $20 + Supabase $25). Trigger.dev dropped from v1.
decisions.reasoning: REVISED per FOUND-02 (two-service stack). v1 fixed costs ~$45/mo (Vercel $20 + Supabase $25). Trigger.dev dropped — scheduling via Vercel cron + agent_triggers DB table. Original $55-75/mo included Trigger.dev ~$10-30 which no longer applies.

decisions.description: Per-client infrastructure cost: 10 clients ~$7-9/client/mo, 50 clients ~$2-3/client/mo, 100 clients ~$1.50-1.80/client/mo. Well under $20 target.
decisions.reasoning: Acknowledged. Per-client cost curve well under $20 target at all projected volumes. Economies of scale from shared infra.

decisions.description: v1.5 (50-500 clients): artifact hosting → R2 + Workers, storage tiering (large files → R2, small files stay Supabase), sandbox snapshots + pre-warming
decisions.reasoning: Future milestone. At ~50 clients: R2 + Workers for artifacts, storage tiering, sandbox pre-warming. Not v1.

decisions.description: v2+ (500+ clients): evaluate Trigger.dev self-hosting, Supabase Team/Enterprise plan, read replicas, regional deployment (only if latency justifies)
decisions.reasoning: Future milestone. At ~500+ clients: evaluate Trigger.dev self-hosting, Supabase enterprise, read replicas, regional deployment. Way out.

decisions.description: Sandbox cost at 100 clients: ~$0.80/client/mo (50 interactions/day × 30% sandbox rate × ~45s avg × $0.128/CPU-hr)
decisions.reasoning: Acknowledged. Sandbox cost ~$0.80/client/mo at 100 clients. Not the cost bottleneck. LLM usage is the real variable cost.

decisions.description: No eval harness built yet — 24 acceptance criteria defined in V1 spec but no automated test infrastructure. Biggest operational gap vs Fintool (2,000+ test cases).
decisions.reasoning: Acknowledged gap. 24 acceptance criteria defined, zero automated tests. Biggest operational gap vs Fintool. Building eval harness is a priority once core product is functional.

decisions.description: Priority eval areas (ordered): CRM accuracy, approval gate correctness, skill-eval pairing, adversarial grounding (fake data injection), auto-filed issues on production errors
decisions.reasoning: Priority order: CRM accuracy → approval gate correctness → skill-eval pairing → adversarial grounding → auto-filed production errors. Safety-critical tests (approval gates) near top.

decisions.description: REVISED per FOUND-02: Observability stack — Vercel logs (functions + cron scanner), per-run telemetry in Supabase (usage_telemetry table), runs table for background job history. No Braintrust/Datadog equivalent in v1.
decisions.reasoning: REVISED per FOUND-02 (Trigger.dev dropped). v1 observability: Vercel logs for all function execution (chat, webhook, cron scanner, trigger runs) + per-run telemetry in Supabase usage_telemetry table (model, tokens, cost, latency per LLM-10) + runs table for background job history and retry tracking (TRIG-04/12). Good enough to debug. No Braintrust/Datadog equivalent yet — revisit when operational volume justifies.

decisions.description: Operational considerations — per-client health views, daily internal health digest, run-level logs with failure classification, fast rollback switches per integration, support runbook
decisions.reasoning: Aspirational ops maturity. Per-client health views, daily digest, failure classification, rollback switches, support runbook. Build incrementally as product matures.

decisions.description: No deal-thesis/contact-thesis pattern — living {deal}-analysis.md with drift detection against market changes. Noted for v2.
decisions.reasoning: Acknowledged. Deal/contact thesis files with drift detection deferred to v2. Not needed for core product value.

decisions.description: Automation Loop partially addressed — synthesis pulse (weekly per TRIG-11) detects patterns across memory files and proposes Growth Plan skill items. Autopilot pulse (~4x/day per TRIG-07/09) nudges user to build unbuilt Growth Plan skills when queue is empty. Full Fintool FDE-style weekly scan loop still deferred to v2/v3, but core detect-propose-build cycle is now in v1 via Growth Plan + pulse integration.
decisions.reasoning: Partially addressed in v1 via Growth Plan + synthesis/autopilot pulses. Full Fintool FDE-style weekly scan loop deferred to v2/v3.

decisions.description: No Outreach Loop — re-engage quiet users with personalized content based on their memory (Fintool FDE loop). Noted for v2/v3.
decisions.reasoning: Acknowledged. Outreach loop for re-engaging quiet users deferred to v2/v3. Retention feature, not activation.

decisions.description: Artifact versioning/rollback not yet designed — DELTA-013 is status 'proposed'. Nightly snapshots + restore script as stopgap.
decisions.reasoning: v1 stopgap: nightly snapshots + restore script. Proper versioning/rollback design deferred. DELTA-013 remains proposed.

decisions.description: RESOLVED by SAFETY-04: Approval matrix defined as two-column rule (internal = auto-run, external-facing = approval required). Per-action granularity deferred to v2 configurable trust levels.
decisions.reasoning: RESOLVED by SAFETY-04. Original gap was 'exact approval matrix per action type — unresolved.' SAFETY-04 resolved this with a simple two-column rule: internal work (CRM reads, memory writes, drafts, research) = auto-run; external-facing work (outbound comms, client-facing CRM changes, deletions) = approval required. Per-action granularity (e.g., 'always auto-approve emails to this contact') deferred to v2 configurable trust levels.

decisions.description: Threshold for introducing second routing profile — unresolved question in V1 spec
decisions.reasoning: Unresolved. Threshold for second routing profile decided during implementation based on observed usage patterns.

decisions.description: Final pricing vs usage limits packaging — unresolved question in V1 spec
decisions.reasoning: Unresolved. Final pricing/usage packaging deferred to pre-launch. Depends on real cost data from beta.

decisions.description: Exact depth of voice output in Wave 1 — unresolved question in V1 spec
decisions.reasoning: Moot — voice deferred to v2 entirely (SERVICE-06/07). No Wave 1 voice depth to decide.

decisions.description: Precise criteria and timing for messaging channel launches — v2: Telegram (via Vercel AI SDK Chat SDK adapter), v3: WhatsApp (via Meta Cloud API). Criteria and timing unresolved.
decisions.reasoning: Channel roadmap updated: v2 Telegram via Vercel AI SDK Chat SDK (shipped, low-friction adapter), v3 WhatsApp via Meta Cloud API (requires Meta Business API approval, 24hr window rules, template management). Telegram first because SDK adapter already exists and no business approval process needed.

decisions.description: Verified retention window and cross-session guarantees for toolcall artifacts — unresolved question in V1 spec
decisions.reasoning: Unresolved. Toolcall artifact retention window TBD during implementation. Start with 7 days, adjust based on storage costs.

decisions.description: Platform limits and ordering guarantees for task list size — unresolved question in V1 spec
decisions.reasoning: Unresolved. Task list size limits and ordering guarantees TBD. Not a problem until scale — standard Supabase pagination handles it.

decisions.description: Memory scope policy at launch: shared across all threads by default vs optional private-thread mode — unresolved question in V1 spec
decisions.reasoning: Shared memory by default. One MEMORY.md per user across all threads. Private-thread mode deferred — add if users request thread isolation.

decisions.description: Bash vs SQL eval study — should run equivalent of Fintool's study: CRM queries via direct SDK vs agent-written SQL vs natural language, search strategies, enrichment approaches
decisions.reasoning: Acknowledged. Bash vs SQL eval study to run when eval harness is built (EVAL-01). Informs tool architecture for CRM queries.

decisions.description: Skills marketplace and community workflows — Shortcut AI's skills library pattern shows skills-as-moat: community-created domain workflows with install counts as trust signals, compounding adoption. V1 has bounded customization + two-tier skills (system/custom) but no discovery, sharing, or community layer. Tracked as V3 Growth Pillar D. Ref: references/Fintool/nicbustamante-reverse-engineering-excel-ai-agents-FULL.md
decisions.reasoning: Acknowledged. Skills marketplace/community deferred to v3 (Growth Pillar D). v1 has system/custom two-tier skills. No discovery, sharing, or community layer yet.

decisions.description: No approval timeout — SAFETY-02 pending approvals in trigger threads sit indefinitely if user doesn't check. No auto-cancel, no escalation. Consider: auto-cancel after 24h with logged explanation in thread.
decisions.reasoning: Acknowledged. v1 accepts stale approvals. Low risk at <50 clients with email notifications (TRIG-06b). Add 24h auto-cancel in v1.5 if stale approvals become a pattern.

decisions.description: No per-client rate limiting — no throttling on API calls per client. One aggressive user could consume disproportionate resources.
decisions.reasoning: Acknowledged. Not needed at <50 clients. Add simple token-bucket rate limiting in v1.5 if cost data shows disproportionate usage. usage_telemetry table (LLM-10) provides monitoring data to detect this.

decisions.description: 15-min Vercel Fluid Compute hard ceiling is the single execution boundary for all agent runs (EXEC-02). No fallback exists — if a run exceeds 15 min it is killed. No Trigger.dev safety net until v3.
decisions.reasoning: Accepted risk. Current v1 workload analysis (FOUND-02) shows nothing approaching 15 min — parallel tool calls keep even 'research 20 leads' under 5 min, typical chat completes in 5-30 seconds. Mitigation: (1) runs table logs duration so we can monitor p95/p99, (2) agent system prompt instructs chunking for large tasks, (3) Trigger.dev in v3 provides genuine >15 min execution for managed scraping pipelines. If any v1 workflow consistently hits >10 min, escalate to Trigger.dev integration before v3.
